title: Social Class
examTip: When asked to 'evaluate' a theory of stratification, always include a named sociologist critic — examiners reward specifically named evidence, not vague phrases like 'some sociologists argue.'
introTips: Social class affects almost every life outcome — health, income, education, housing and even how long you live.
The way we measure class has changed hugely — the old upper/middle/working split has been replaced by more detailed systems.
Named sociologists and real statistics are your best friends in exam answers on this topic.
intro: Your postcode, your parents' jobs, your accent — these things should have nothing to do with how long you live or how far you go in life. Yet social class remains one of the strongest predictors of life chances in the UK. Understanding how class works, how we measure it, and why it still matters is at the heart of the sociology of stratification.

keyPoints.heading: Defining and Measuring Social Class
keyPoints.content: A social class is a group of people who share a similar economic position in society. The traditional model divides society into three broad classes: the upper class (inherited wealth, land and capital), the middle class (non-manual workers — teachers, doctors, managers), and the working class (manual workers — miners, factory workers, cleaners). However this simple picture has become harder to apply. The modern official measure is the NS-SEC (National Statistics Socio-Economic Classification), which places people into 8 categories based on employment relations — things like how much autonomy you have at work and whether you have a salary or an hourly wage. Even the NS-SEC has problems: occupations shift in status over time (is a nurse working class or middle class?), women were historically classified by their husband's job, and the traditional manual working class has shrunk as deindustrialisation removed factory and mining jobs.
writeBullets: Social class — a group sharing a similar economic position in society
Upper class — inherited wealth, land and capital (old money)
Middle class — non-manual workers: teachers, doctors, managers, professionals
Working class — manual workers: miners, factory workers, construction workers
NS-SEC — modern 8-category system based on employment relations and autonomy
Problems: occupational change, historical gender bias, shrinking manual WC

keyTerms.def: A group sharing a similar economic position in society
National Statistics Socio-Economic Classification — the UK's official 8-class system based on employment relations
Physical, hands-on labour — traditionally associated with working-class occupations
Office, professional or managerial work — traditionally associated with middle-class occupations

keyPoints.memTip: NS-SEC = 'Not So Simple, Even for Classifying' — it has 8 categories because class is complicated.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often use 'working class' loosely — push them to use NS-SEC language in exam answers and flag that the system changes over time.

keyPoints.heading: Life Chances
keyPoints.content: Life chances is a term coined by Max Weber to describe the likelihood of obtaining desirable outcomes — good health, high income, quality education, job security and safe housing. Social class predicts life chances more powerfully than almost any other factor. Health: men in the highest class (Class 1) live on average 7.4 years longer than men in the lowest class (Class 7, ONS data). Wealth and income: the top 10% of households own 45% of UK wealth, while the bottom 50% own just 9% (ONS, 2022). Education: class background predicts educational attainment at every level — covered in detail in Topic S2. Housing: working-class households are far more likely to rent privately or live in social housing; poor and overcrowded housing is directly linked to worse health outcomes. Criminal justice: working-class people are overrepresented in the prison population — 74% of prisoners left school with no qualifications (Ministry of Justice).
writeBullets: Life chances (Weber) — the likelihood of obtaining desirable outcomes in life
Health gap — Class 1 men live 7.4 years longer than Class 7 men (ONS)
Wealth gap — top 10% own 45% of UK wealth; bottom 50% own just 9%
Housing — working-class households more likely to rent; poor housing damages health
Criminal justice — 74% of prisoners left school with no qualifications
Education — class predicts achievement at every stage (see Topic S2)

keyTerms.def: Max Weber's term for the likelihood of achieving desirable outcomes — health, wealth, education, security
German sociologist (1864–1920) who developed the concept of life chances and a multi-dimensional view of stratification

keyPoints.memTip: Weber's 'life chances' — think of it as a lottery ticket: your class determines how many tickets you start with.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often say 'the rich are healthier' without naming Weber — train them to link the concept explicitly.

keyPoints.heading: Changes in Class Structure
keyPoints.content: The class structure of the UK has changed dramatically since the 1960s. Deindustrialisation — the decline of coal, steel and shipbuilding from the 1970s — destroyed the economic base of the traditional working class and fragmented working-class identity. At the same time, the growth of the service sector expanded the middle class. The embourgeoisement thesis asked whether better-paid working-class workers were becoming middle class in their values and politics. Goldthorpe and Lockwood's 'Affluent Worker' study (1969) tested this in Luton: they found that while affluent manual workers did share some middle-class lifestyle traits (car ownership, home ownership), they remained culturally and politically working class — collectivist, voting Labour, identifying as working class. The thesis was rejected. The underclass debate concerns the most disadvantaged group at the bottom of the class structure. Charles Murray (1984, New Right) blamed welfare dependency and worklessness for creating a 'culture of poverty.' Critics Bagguley and Mann (1992) argued this stigmatises the poor and ignores structural causes of poverty such as unemployment and low wages.
writeBullets: Deindustrialisation — decline of coal, steel and shipbuilding from 1970s, shrinking traditional WC
Embourgeoisement thesis — do richer WC workers become MC in values and politics?
Goldthorpe and Lockwood (1969) 'Affluent Worker' — WC kept collectivist identity despite higher wages
Embourgeoisement thesis rejected — lifestyle similarities ≠ class identity change
Underclass — Murray (New Right): welfare dependency creates a culture of poverty
Bagguley and Mann — Murray stigmatises the poor; ignores structural causes like low wages

keyTerms.def: The decline of manufacturing and heavy industry, especially from the 1970s, which destroyed the economic base of the traditional working class
The idea that affluent working-class workers adopt middle-class values and identities
A group at the very bottom of the class structure, seen by Murray as welfare-dependent, and by critics as structurally disadvantaged
Goldthorpe and Lockwood's 1969 Luton study which rejected the embourgeoisement thesis

keyPoints.memTip: Goldthorpe and Lockwood in Luton — 'Loaded workers, Labour voters' — they had money but stayed working class.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions on class change are common — ensure students can evaluate Murray with Bagguley and Mann.

keyPoints.heading: Class Identity Today
keyPoints.content: The most ambitious recent study of class is Mike Savage's 'Social Class in the 21st Century' (2015), based on the Great British Class Survey (161,000 respondents). Rather than three classes, Savage identified seven classes: the elite, established middle class, technical middle class, new affluent workers, traditional working class, emergent service workers, and the precariat — the most deprived group, characterised by insecure work, low income and little cultural engagement. The postmodern view, associated with Pakulski and Waters (1996, 'The Death of Class'), argues that class no longer determines identity in the way it once did — identity is now built through consumption, lifestyle, gender and ethnicity. However, Savage's own data contradicts this: class background still strongly predicts life chances even if people do not identify strongly with a class label. The tension between class as a lived identity and class as a structural position is central to contemporary sociological debate.
writeBullets: Savage (2015) Great British Class Survey — 161,000 respondents, found 7 classes not 3
Precariat — most deprived class: insecure work, low pay, limited cultural engagement
Postmodern view — Pakulski and Waters (1996): class identity replaced by consumption and lifestyle
Savage's criticism — class still predicts life chances even if identity is fluid
Class identity vs class structure — people may not feel 'working class' but still face its disadvantages

keyTerms.def: The most deprived class in Savage's model — characterised by insecure work, low income and little cultural capital
Mike Savage's 2011–2013 survey of 161,000 people that identified 7 distinct social classes in the UK
The view that grand structures like class no longer determine identity — replaced by individual consumption and lifestyle choices
British sociologist who led the Great British Class Survey (2015) — argued class still matters structurally

keyPoints.memTip: Savage vs Pakulski: Savage says class STRUCTURE still matters even when class IDENTITY fades.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often misread postmodernism as saying inequality doesn't exist — clarify: it says identity is fluid, NOT that inequality has gone.

tables.title: Social Class: Categories, Life Chances and Sociological Views
rows: Marxists: bourgeoisie exploit through ownership of means of production
Longer life expectancy, high educational attainment
Functionalists: rewarded for important, skilled roles (Davis & Moore, 1945)
Class 7 men live 7.4 years less than Class 1 (ONS)
Marxists: proletariat exploited; Weberians: poor life chances across all domains
Insecure income, poor housing, limited social/cultural capital
Postmodernists: deny class exists; Savage: precariat faces real structural disadvantage
New Right: cultural explanation; Bagguley & Mann: structural causes ignored

0.question: What did Goldthorpe and Lockwood's 'Affluent Worker' study (1969) find about the embourgeoisement thesis?
options: Affluent working-class workers kept their working-class identity and political values
Better-paid workers had fully adopted middle-class values and voted Conservative
The study confirmed that higher wages automatically lead to middle-class identity
Workers in Luton identified as middle class because of their car ownership
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. Despite sharing lifestyle traits like car ownership, the Luton workers remained collectivist, voted Labour and identified as working class — so the thesis was rejected.
0.wrongFeedback: Not quite. Goldthorpe and Lockwood found that higher wages did NOT turn workers into middle-class voters or change their identity — the embourgeoisement thesis was rejected.

2.question: Which of the following best describes the term 'life chances' as used by Max Weber?
options: The likelihood of achieving desirable outcomes such as health, income and education
The number of job opportunities available to someone in the working class
The chance that a person will move up the class structure through hard work
The probability of surviving childhood based on parental income
2.correctFeedback: Spot on. Weber used 'life chances' broadly — it covers health, income, education, housing and security. Class is the key predictor.
2.wrongFeedback: Close, but Weber's concept is broader than just jobs. Life chances covers all desirable outcomes — health, wealth, education, housing — and class predicts all of them.

3.question: Mike Savage's Great British Class Survey (2015) identified how many social classes?
3.correctFeedback: Correct — seven, ranging from the elite to the precariat. This challenged the traditional upper/middle/working class model.
3.wrongFeedback: The traditional model has three classes, but Savage's survey found seven — including new categories like the precariat and the technical middle class.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'class' with 'income' — class includes wealth, status and employment relations, not just how much you earn this month.
Saying the embourgeoisement thesis was 'supported' — Goldthorpe and Lockwood actually rejected it after their Luton study.
Using 'working class' as if it just means 'poor' — the NS-SEC defines class by employment relations, not income alone.
Thinking postmodernism means inequality has disappeared — Pakulski and Waters say class identity has declined, not that everyone is equal.
Forgetting that Murray's underclass thesis is contested — always pair it with Bagguley and Mann's structural critique.
examTips: In 'explain' questions, use the formula: point → named sociologist/evidence → explain how it links to the question.
For 'evaluate' questions on class theories, use contrasting perspectives: e.g., Davis and Moore vs Tumin, or Murray vs Bagguley and Mann.
Statistics hit hard in sociology answers — the 7.4-year life expectancy gap and the 45%/9% wealth split are both exam-ready facts.
Always link class to life chances — health, housing, education and CJS outcomes are all class-related and all examinable.
Don't just list the NS-SEC categories — show you understand why measuring class is difficult (gender bias, occupational change).
revisionCardBullets: Social class — group sharing similar economic position
Traditional model: upper (inherited wealth), middle (non-manual), working (manual)
NS-SEC — modern 8-category official classification based on employment relations
Life chances (Weber) — likelihood of obtaining desirable outcomes: health, wealth, education
Class 1 men live 7.4 years longer than Class 7 men (ONS)
Top 10% own 45% of UK wealth; bottom 50% own just 9%
Embourgeoisement thesis — Goldthorpe & Lockwood (1969) rejected it: Luton workers stayed WC
Deindustrialisation — shrunk traditional WC from 1970s as coal, steel, shipbuilding declined
Underclass — Murray (New Right): welfare dependency; Bagguley & Mann: structural causes ignored
Savage (2015) Great British Class Survey — 7 classes including the precariat
Precariat — most deprived: insecure work, low income, little cultural engagement
Pakulski & Waters (1996): class identity replaced by consumption — Savage disagrees
